Yes. 

Now, putting all together. 

Now, considering possible issues. 

What about cycles? For example, a node that can loop between red and blue edges. But since we track visited (distance is set once, and not processed again), the BFS will not loop infinitely. 

What about multiple edges? Since the BFS takes the first path to reach a state, and that's the shortest, multiple edges are handled. 

Now, code:

Implementing all this in the starter code. 

So the code would be:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> shortestAlternatingPaths(int n, vector<vector<int>>& redEdges, vector<vector<int>>& blueEdges) {
        vector<vector<int>> adj_red(n);
        vector<vector<int>> adj_blue(n);
        
        for (auto& e : redEdges) {
            adj_red[e[0]].push_back(e[1]);
        }
        
        for (auto& e : blueEdges) {
            adj_blue[e[0]].push_back(e[1]);
        }
        
        vector<vector<int>> dist(n, vector<int>{-1, -1});
        queue<pair<int, int>> q;
        
        q.push({0, 0}); // 0: red, next color is red
        q.push({0, 1}); // 1: blue, next color is blue?
        dist[0][0] = 0;
        dist[0][1] = 0;
        
        while (!q.empty()) {
            auto [u, color] = q.front();
            q.pop();
            
            vector<int> neighbors = (color == 0) ? adj_red[u] : adj_blue[u];
            for (int v : neighbors) {
                int next_color = 1 - color;
                if (dist[v][next_color] == -1) {
                    dist[v][next_color] = dist[u][color] + 1;
                    q.push({v, next_color});
                }
            }
        }
        
        vector<int> answer(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            int min_dist = INT_MAX;
            if (dist[i][0] != -1) min_dist = min(min_dist, dist[i][0]);
            if (dist[i][1] != -1) min_dist = min(min_dist, dist[i][1]);
            answer[i] = (min_dist == INT_MAX) ? -1 : min_dist;
        }
        
        return answer;
    }
};

Now, putting all together. 

Now, considering possible issues. 

What about cycles? For example, a node that can loop between red and blue edges. But since we track visited (distance is set once, and not processed again), the BFS will not loop infinitely. 

What about multiple edges? Since the BFS takes the first path to reach a state, and that's the shortest, multiple edges are handled. 

Now, code:

Implementing all this in the starter code. 

So the code would be:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> shortestAlternatingPaths(int n, vector<vector<int>>& redEdges, vector<vector<int>>& blueEdges) {
        vector<vector<int>> adj_red(n);
        vector<vector<int>> adj_blue(n);
        
        for (auto& e : redEdges) {
            adj_red[e[0]].push_back(e[1]);
        }
        
        for (auto& e : blueEdges) {
            adj_blue[e[0]].push_back(e[1]);
        }
        
        vector<vector<int>> dist(n, vector<int>{-1, -1});
        queue<pair<int, int>> q;
        
        q.push({0, 0}); // 0: red, next color is red
        q.push({0, 1}); // 1: blue, next color is blue?
        dist[0][0] = 0;
        dist[0][1] = 0;
        
        while (!q.empty()) {
            auto [u, color] = q.front();
            q.pop();
            
            vector<int> neighbors = (color == 0) ? adj_red[u] : adj_blue[u];
            for (int v : neighbors) {
                int next_color = 1 - color;
                if (dist[v][next_color] == -1) {
                    dist[v][next_color] = dist[u][color] + 1;
                    q.push({v, next_color});
                }
            }
        }
        
        vector<int> answer(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            int min_dist = INT_MAX;
            if (dist[i][0] != -1) min_dist = min(min_dist, dist[i][0]);
            if (dist[i][1] != -1) min_dist = min(min_dist, dist[i][1]);
            answer[i] = (min_dist == INT_MAX) ? -1 : min_dist;
        }
        
        return answer;
    }
};
